aliases.name: The Good Neighbours
aliases.meaning: euphemism to avoid offending the Fae

description.summary: Fairies are a class of supernatural beings in European folklore, particularly Celtic, Germanic and English traditions, dwelling in an Otherworld that overlaps with our own and capable of magic, abduction, and exchange of changelings.
description.full: Fairies (also Fae, Fair Folk, Aos Sí, Sidhe, Tylwyth Teg) are a broad class of supernatural beings central to European, especially Celtic and British, folklore. Tradition imagines them as a separate race occupying an Otherworld that interpenetrates our own through fairy mounds (sídhe), wells, and liminal places. The folkloric Fae are not the diminutive winged figures of Victorian art but powerful, ambivalent beings: capable of beauty and benevolence, but also of abduction, theft, and the substitution of human infants with changelings. Folk explanations of their origin range from demoted angels and the fallen Tuatha Dé Danann to spirits of the dead, hidden races, and elementals. Belief in fairies was widespread in Britain and Ireland into the modern period, sustained by texts including Robert Kirk's 1691 manuscript The Secret Commonwealth of Elves, Fauns and Fairies and a continuous oral tradition. The phenomenon experienced a literary efflorescence in the Renaissance (Spenser, Shakespeare) and a Victorian sentimentalisation (J. M. Barrie, the Cottingley Fairies hoax of 1917) before being recovered in serious folkloristic and Celtic-revival scholarship in the 20th century.

etymology.origin: The English 'fairy' derives from Old French faerie ('enchantment, the realm of the fays'), from Latin fata ('the Fates'). The Old French was originally a collective noun reinterpreted as a singular from the 14th century. Celtic-language terms (sídhe, daoine sí, tylwyth teg) are independent and older.
etymology.meaning: 'Fairy' originally denoted both the enchanted state and the inhabitants of the realm of the fays. 'Sídhe' literally names a fairy mound and by extension its inhabitants. Anglicised 'Sidhe' or 'Shee' are common in 19th- and 20th-century Irish writing.
etymology.first_use: c. 14th century (English 'fairy'); much earlier in Old Irish (síd) and Welsh (tylwyth teg).

taxonomy.variant: Pan-European, with strong Celtic and British concentrations

related_phenomena.name: Changeling
related_phenomena.description: Fae infant substituted for a stolen human child

related_phenomena.name: Banshee
related_phenomena.description: Bean sí ('woman of the fairy mound'), a death-announcing fairy of Irish tradition

related_phenomena.name: Brownie
related_phenomena.description: Household fairy of Scottish and English tradition

related_phenomena.name: Huldufólk
related_phenomena.description: Icelandic 'hidden people' parallel to British fae

related_phenomena.name: Elves
related_phenomena.description: Germanic and Norse supernatural beings often conflated with fairies

features.description: Most folkloric fairies are described as human in shape and proportion, often beautiful, sometimes with green eyes or a slight 'glamour' that distinguishes them
Sizes range from very small to fully human; the diminutive flower-fairy is largely a Victorian-era literary development
Wings are rare in older folklore and primarily a feature of Victorian artwork (e.g. Richard Doyle, Arthur Rackham)
Often described as preternaturally beautiful, sometimes betrayed by a small physical anomaly such as cloven feet, hollow backs, or reversed feet

physical.morphology: Folkloric fairies are typically anthropomorphic and pass for human at a distance. The Victorian 'tiny winged fairy' is a literary and artistic convention; older British and Irish fairies are usually full-sized, glamour-cloaked beings of the Otherworld.

abilities.description: Power to alter the perception of mortals, making fairy gold, food, or palaces seem real
Time in the fairy realm runs differently; classic tales (e.g. Oisín, Rip Van Winkle analogues) describe travelers returning after centuries
Fairies are reported to take human infants (replaced with changelings), brides, midwives, and musicians
Many fairy beings (e.g. selkies, kelpies) are explicitly shapeshifting
Fairies are widely reported to bless or curse households depending on hospitality and respect

habitat_preference: liminal seasonal sites at Samhain and Bealtaine
behavior.diet: Often described as drinking milk and dew, eating bread, oatmeal, or fairy fruit. Sharing fairy food with mortals is taboo and may bind the eater to the Otherworld.
patterns: Most active at twilight and at the boundary festivals of Samhain and Bealtaine
Travel along fairy paths that must not be obstructed
Take offence at insult or trespass, reward respect and generosity
Seek out skilled human midwives, musicians, and craftsmen

range.description: Fairy belief is centred in the Celtic-speaking regions of Ireland, Scotland, the Isle of Man, Wales, Cornwall, and Brittany, with extensive parallel traditions across Germanic and Scandinavian Europe and surviving Anglo-American folk belief in Newfoundland and Appalachia.

hotspots.name: Brú na Bóinne / Newgrange

location.description: Neolithic passage tomb complex in the Boyne valley, identified in tradition as a sídh and home of Aengus and the Dagda

hotspots.notes: One of the most important sídhe in Irish mythology

hotspots.name: Knockma (Cnoc Meadha)

location.description: Hill identified in tradition as the seat of Finvarra, king of the Connacht fairies

hotspots.notes: Traditional capital of the Connacht aos sí

location.description: Geological landscape near Uig long associated in local tradition with the daoine sìth

first_recorded.source: Early medieval Irish ecclesiastical and mythological texts (e.g. 7th-c. Tírechán; 8th-c. Fiacc's Hymn) describe the aos sí

peak_activity_periods.description: Medieval romance and Celtic mythological literature: Tam Lin, Thomas the Rhymer, Le Morte d'Arthur
Renaissance and early-modern literary fairies: Spenser's Faerie Queene (1590); Shakespeare's A Midsummer Night's Dream (c. 1595); Robert Kirk's Secret Commonwealth (1691)
Victorian and Edwardian fairy revival, Cottingley Fairies (1917)

temporal.seasonal_patterns: Fairy activity is associated above all with the cross-quarter festivals of Samhain (1 November) and Bealtaine (1 May), the liminal hours of twilight, and the white nights around midsummer.

origins.cultural_roots: Fairy belief in the Celtic world is generally traced to pre-Christian Irish and British religion, particularly the Tuatha Dé Danann tradition recorded in the 11th-century Lebor Gabála Érenn. After Christianisation, the older deities were re-imagined as the aos sí, retreating beneath the land into the sídhe.
origins.earliest_accounts: Early medieval Irish ecclesiastical texts already discuss the sídhe as 'earthly gods'; Tírechán (7th c.) and Fiacc's Hymn (8th c.) reference pre-Christian veneration of these beings. Gervase of Tilbury's Otia Imperialia (c. 1211) is among the earliest detailed Latin accounts of English fairies.
origins.folklore_connections: Fairies belong to a wider Indo-European complex of 'hidden people' beings, including the Norse álfar, the Icelandic huldufólk, the Germanic elves, the Slavic vily, and the diwata of the Philippines.

timeline.event: 7th-c. bishop Tírechán describes the aos sí as 'earthly gods'
timeline.significance: Earliest surviving Christian description of Irish fairies
timeline.source: Tírechán, in the Book of Armagh

timeline.event: Gervase of Tilbury includes English fairy lore in Otia Imperialia
timeline.significance: Foundational Latin documentation of British fairies

timeline.event: Morgan le Fay is named in Thomas Malory's Le Morte d'Arthur
timeline.significance: Major literary appearance of a fae figure in Arthurian legend

timeline.event: Edmund Spenser publishes books I–III of The Faerie Queene
King James VI publishes Daemonologie, treating fairies as illusory spirits
Reverend Robert Kirk completes The Secret Commonwealth of Elves, Fauns and Fairies
timeline.significance: Foundational ethnographic survey of Scottish fairy belief

timeline.event: Thomas Crofton Croker publishes Fairy Legends and Traditions of the South of Ireland
timeline.significance: Key 19th-century collection of Irish fairy folklore

timeline.event: W. B. Yeats edits Fairy and Folk Tales of the Irish Peasantry
timeline.significance: Brings Irish fairy tradition into the literary mainstream of the Celtic Revival

timeline.event: Frances Griffiths and Elsie Wright produce the first Cottingley Fairies photographs in Yorkshire
timeline.significance: Most famous fairy photo hoax; admitted as faked by the cousins in 1983
timeline.source: Cottingley Fairies; Doyle, A. C. (1922) The Coming of the Fairies

timeline.event: Arthur Conan Doyle publishes The Coming of the Fairies, defending the Cottingley photographs
timeline.significance: Pivotal moment in modern public engagement with fairy belief

timeline.event: Katharine Briggs publishes A Dictionary of Fairies
timeline.significance: Standard reference work on British fairy tradition

history.modern_era: Contemporary engagement with fairy belief takes three main forms: serious folkloristic study (Briggs, Narváez, Wilby), surviving local belief (notably Iceland's huldufólk, Newfoundland 'fairy paths', and Irish hawthorn taboos that have stopped roads from being built into the 21st century), and commercial fairy imagery in children's literature, film, and tourism.

notable.name: Robert Kirk's Aberfoyle ministry

location.description: Aberfoyle, Stirling, in the Scottish Highlands

types: Reverend Robert Kirk (Episcopal minister and Gaelic scholar)

notable.description: Robert Kirk, minister of Aberfoyle, completed his manuscript The Secret Commonwealth of Elves, Fauns and Fairies, drawing on Highland informants and his own observation of local fairy belief. He reportedly fell dead on the Doon Hill 'fairy hill' the following year; local tradition said he had been taken into the sídh.
sources: Kirk, R. (1691, publ. 1815), The Secret Commonwealth

notable.name: Cottingley Fairies photographs

location.description: Cottingley Beck, behind the Wright family home in Cottingley village near Bradford

credibility_factors: Endorsement by Arthur Conan Doyle in The Strand Magazine, 1920

notable.description: Cousins Frances Griffiths and Elsie Wright produced five photographs purporting to show fairies at Cottingley Beck. The first two were taken in July 1917; further photographs followed in 1920. Arthur Conan Doyle defended the photographs publicly in 1920–1922.

factors: Cousins admitted in 1983 that four of the five photographs were faked using paper cut-outs of figures from Princess Mary's Gift Book
Frances Griffiths maintained until her death that the fifth was genuine
credibility.notes: The most famous fairy photo case; long debate, definitive admission of hoax in 1983.

sources: Doyle, A. C. (1922), The Coming of the Fairies
Cooper, J. (1990), The Case of the Cottingley Fairies

photographic.name: Cottingley Fairies photographs
photographic.description: Five photographs taken by Frances Griffiths and Elsie Wright at Cottingley Beck, West Yorkshire, depicting alleged fairies and a gnome.
photographic.analysis: In 1983 the cousins admitted that the figures were paper cut-outs based on illustrations from Princess Mary's Gift Book (1914). Griffiths continued to insist the fifth photograph was genuine.
photographic.source: Cooper, J. (1990), The Case of the Cottingley Fairies

documentary.name: The Secret Commonwealth of Elves, Fauns and Fairies
documentary.description: Manuscript ethnographic account of Highland fairy belief by Reverend Robert Kirk of Aberfoyle.
documentary.analysis: First-hand minister's account compiled from Highland informants; first published by Walter Scott in 1815.

paranormal.name: Fairies as a separate sentient race
paranormal.description: Folk and esoteric tradition holds that the Fae are a real, autonomous race of intelligent beings inhabiting an Otherworld that interpenetrates the physical world.
proponents: W. Y. Evans-Wentz (The Fairy-Faith in Celtic Countries, 1911)
evidence_cited: Continuity of independent witness accounts across centuries
Persistence of belief in modern Iceland and rural Ireland

paranormal.name: Spirits of the dead
paranormal.description: Fairies represent ancestral spirits or the unbaptised dead, particularly those who died untimely or in childbirth.
evidence_cited: Overlap of fairy traditions with All Souls' / Samhain rites

scientific.name: Cultural memory of pre-existing peoples
scientific.description: The Fae may preserve cultural memory of earlier or marginalised populations of the British Isles re-imagined as a hidden race.
evidence_cited: Association of fairy mounds with Neolithic and Bronze Age burial sites

scientific.name: Folk explanation of medical and developmental conditions
scientific.description: Changeling beliefs encoded folk explanations of conditions including failure to thrive, autism, and developmental disorders. The Bridget Cleary case (Ireland, 1895) is a tragic legal exemplar.
evidence_cited: 1895 killing of Bridget Cleary, an adult woman accused of being a changeling, in Tipperary

skeptical.name: Cultural and literary construct
skeptical.description: Fairies are an evolving cultural construct shaped by medieval romance, early-modern literature, Victorian sentimentalism, and modern children's media.
evidence_cited: The shift from full-sized folkloric Fae to tiny winged Victorian fairies

traditions: Scottish Seelie and Unseelie Court tradition
stories: Thomas the Rhymer (Scottish ballad, child ballad 37)
Bridget Cleary (1895) — folk-belief homicide case in Tipperary
folklore.beliefs: Across Ireland, Scotland and Wales, traditional belief insists that fairies are real, dangerous to offend, and best treated with euphemism, generosity, and respect. Hawthorn 'fairy trees' must not be cut, fairy paths must not be built across, and milk and bread are left out as offerings.

literature: A Midsummer Night's Dream — William Shakespeare (c. 1595)
The Secret Commonwealth — Robert Kirk (1691, publ. 1815)
Fairy Legends and Traditions of the South of Ireland — Thomas Crofton Croker (1828)
Fairy and Folk Tales of the Irish Peasantry — W. B. Yeats (1888)
A Dictionary of Fairies — Katharine Briggs (1976)
At the Bottom of the Garden — Diane Purkiss (2000)
film_tv: Darby O'Gill and the Little People (1959)
Fairy Tale: A True Story (1997, on Cottingley)
games: The Legend of Zelda series (fairies as healing spirits)
Dungeons & Dragons (Feywild and fey creature taxonomy)
music: Numerous Child ballads (Tam Lin, Thomas the Rhymer)
other: Hawthorn-tree preservation in Irish road planning (e.g. the Latoon fairy bush, 1999)

researchers.name: Reverend Robert Kirk
researchers.contribution: The Secret Commonwealth (1691); foundational Highland ethnography of fairy belief

researchers.name: W. B. Yeats
researchers.contribution: Compiled and popularised Irish fairy folklore

researchers.name: Katharine Briggs
researchers.contribution: A Dictionary of Fairies (1976); standard reference on British fairies

researchers.name: Diane Purkiss
researchers.contribution: At the Bottom of the Garden (2000); cultural history of fairies and changelings

researchers.name: Emma Wilby
researchers.contribution: Cunning Folk and Familiar Spirits (2005); academic work on fairy familiars in early-modern witch trials

organizations.name: Folklore Society
organizations.focus: British academic folklore studies, including fairy traditions

organizations.name: Fairy Investigation Society
organizations.focus: Originally founded 1927; revived in 2013 as a forum for collecting modern fairy reports

research.ongoing_studies: Active areas include the historical-ethnographic study of fairy belief in early-modern witch trials, surviving Icelandic huldufólk belief, modern fairy-encounter surveys (Simon Young's 2014–2017 census), and Celtic-revival reception studies.

sources.title: The Secret Commonwealth of Elves, Fauns and Fairies
At the Bottom of the Garden: A Dark History of Fairies, Hobgoblins, and Other Troublesome Things
Fairy and Folk Tales of the Irish Peasantry
Fairy Legends and Traditions of the South of Ireland
The Case of the Cottingley Fairies
